name: Lua 5.4 — Optional Generational Mode
summary: Optional generational mode (opt-in via collectgarbage('generational')) added alongside the default incremental tricolor collector; not the default because it doesn't help large-data-structure workloads.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Lua 5.4 added an **optional generational mode** (opt-in via `collectgarbage("generational")`) alongside the default incremental tricolor collector — not the default because it doesn't help large-data-structure workloads.

The cleanest example of "generational hypothesis is workload-specific" — Lua makes it opt-in deliberately.

sections.title: Two-cycle aging
sections.content:
**Two-cycle aging** (G_NEW → G_SURVIVAL → G_OLD0/1/G_OLD) — objects must survive *two* GC cycles to become old, fixing the inaccurate single-cycle promotion of the failed Lua 5.2 generational experiment.

Minor / major multipliers (default 20% / 100% of last-major heap) tune frequency; can degrade to incremental temporarily under stress. Forward-barrier driven; old objects touched by a write transition to G_TOUCHED states tracked in a separate gray list.

sections.title: Generational hypothesis is workload-specific
sections.content:
The cleanest example of "generational hypothesis is workload-specific" — Lua makes it opt-in deliberately. Lua 5.2 had tried generational GC as default and reverted because workloads with large persistent data structures (game state, configuration tables) violate the generational hypothesis: most objects do *not* die young in those settings.

The lesson: a runtime that defaults to incremental tricolor and offers generational as an opt-in is more honest than a runtime that defaults to generational and forces large-data-structure workloads to pay promotion costs.
